stem: Which hand tool is used to tighten or loosen nuts and bolts and has an opening that can be adjusted to fit different fastener sizes?
choices: Adjustable wrench
Needle-nose pliers
Cold chisel
Ball-peen hammer
explanation: An adjustable wrench, often called a crescent wrench, has a thumbwheel that slides one jaw in and out so a single tool fits a range of hex nuts and bolts. Needle-nose pliers are for gripping small wires and parts in tight spots and will round the corners of any fastener you try to drive with them. A cold chisel is a striking tool for cutting metal, not for fasteners. A ball-peen hammer is used to shape metal and strike punches. Only the adjustable wrench is designed to set its opening to a fastener size.

stem: A mechanic needs to grip a round pipe firmly while cutting threads onto it. Which tool is designed specifically to hold cylindrical workpieces without slipping?
choices: Slip-joint pliers
Pipe wrench
Box-end wrench
Offset screwdriver
explanation: A pipe wrench has hardened, toothed jaws set at an angle so that pulling on the handle drives the teeth deeper into the round pipe, locking the grip tighter the harder you pull. Slip-joint pliers can hold a pipe but the jaws will rotate around the work under any real torque. A box-end wrench is shaped to wrap a six- or twelve-point hex fastener and will not grip a smooth cylinder at all. An offset screwdriver is for turning screws in cramped spots. Only the pipe wrench is built for round stock.

stem: A torque wrench is used instead of a standard socket wrench when:
choices: you need to reach fasteners in deep recesses
tightening fasteners to a precise torque specification is required
the fastener head is stripped and will not grip
you are working in a tight space and need a ratcheting motion
explanation: A torque wrench has a calibrated mechanism that signals or limits at a preset value, letting you tighten a fastener to the exact specification a manufacturer calls out for head bolts, lug nuts, or connecting rods. Reaching deep recesses is what a socket extension solves, not a torque wrench. A stripped fastener head needs an extractor or vise grips, not torque measurement. Tight-space ratcheting is offered by any ratchet handle, including non-torque ones. The defining feature is the calibrated measurement, not the reach or the ratcheting.

stem: What is the primary purpose of a center punch when working with metal?
choices: To scribe a straight line across the workpiece
To mark a starting point so a drill bit does not wander
To remove burrs from a drilled hole
To cut a hole in sheet metal without a drill
explanation: A center punch is struck with a hammer to dimple the metal at the spot where a hole will be drilled, and that dimple cups the drill point so the bit starts where you intended instead of wandering across the surface. Scribing a straight line is the job of a scriber or a square. Removing burrs from a drilled hole is what a deburring tool or countersink does. Cutting a hole in sheet metal without a drill calls for a hole punch or knockout, not a center punch. Center punches mark, they do not cut or scribe.

stem: A technician selects a drill bit with a twist-style cutting flute and a 118-degree included angle tip. This bit is best suited for drilling:
choices: concrete and masonry
general-purpose holes in steel or aluminum
glass and ceramic tile
large-diameter holes in wood only
explanation: A standard high-speed steel twist bit with a 118-degree tip is the everyday bit for drilling steel, aluminum, and brass, and it is what comes in a typical mechanic's index. Concrete and masonry require a carbide-tipped masonry bit driven by a hammer drill, because HSS cannot cut stone. Glass and ceramic tile need a spear-point or diamond bit and very slow speed to avoid cracking. Wood-only large-diameter holes are the territory of spade bits, Forstner bits, or hole saws. The 118-degree point geometry is the giveaway for general metal work.

stem: A vise-grip (locking pliers) differs from standard slip-joint pliers in that a vise-grip:
choices: can be used as a wrench on hex fasteners only
locks onto a workpiece and maintains clamping force without the user squeezing
is used exclusively for cutting wire
adjusts automatically to the size of the fastener
explanation: Locking pliers, often called Vise-Grips, use an over-center toggle linkage that snaps the jaws shut and holds them there without any hand pressure, freeing both of your hands and applying steady clamping force. Slip-joint pliers and channel locks need a constant squeeze to hold anything. The pliers are not limited to hex fasteners and can grip almost any shape. Wire cutting is a feature of dedicated cutters or a small notch on some pliers, not the defining feature here. They do not auto-adjust to fastener size; the user sets the jaw with a thumbscrew.

stem: A combination square is primarily used to:
choices: apply torque to hex fasteners in tight spaces
measure and mark 90-degree and 45-degree angles on a workpiece
hold a workpiece in place during cutting
thread bolts onto nuts in hard-to-reach locations
explanation: A combination square consists of a rule and a sliding head that provides both 90-degree and 45-degree reference angles. It is used to mark cut lines, check squareness, and verify miters. It does not apply torque or hold workpieces.

stem: A snap-ring pliers set includes two types of tips. One type spreads a ring outward to remove an external snap ring; the other type squeezes a ring inward to remove an internal snap ring. Why must the correct type be used?
choices: Using the wrong type will damage the pliers' handles
Using the wrong type moves the ring in the wrong direction and can launch it unexpectedly or distort it
Both types are interchangeable; choice depends only on ring size
The wrong type will strip the threading on the fastener
explanation: External snap rings are compressed to fit into a shaft groove; internal rings are expanded to fit inside a bore groove. Using expanding pliers on an external ring, or compressing pliers on an internal ring, pushes the ring the wrong way, risking sudden release and possible injury or ring damage.

stem: A mechanic describes a tool as having a T-shaped handle, with a square drive that accepts interchangeable sockets, and a ratcheting pawl that allows rotation in one direction without removing the tool from the fastener. What tool is being described?
choices: Breaker bar
Speed handle
Ratchet wrench
Flex-head torque wrench
explanation: A ratchet wrench has a reversible ratcheting mechanism that lets the user turn the handle back without moving the fastener, then apply torque again on the next forward stroke. A breaker bar provides high torque but does not ratchet; a speed handle spins freely but also lacks a ratchet pawl.

stem: When measuring a shaft's outside diameter, a technician reads a micrometer thimble scale and finds the sleeve reading is 0.350 inches and the thimble adds 0.012 inches. What is the total measured diameter?
choices: 0.338 inches
0.350 inches
0.362 inches
0.412 inches
explanation: A micrometer reading combines the barrel (sleeve) measurement and the thimble graduation. Total = 0.350 + 0.012 = 0.362 inches. Subtracting instead of adding (0.350 − 0.012 = 0.338) is a common error; the thimble always adds to the sleeve reading.

stem: A technician wants to cut an external thread onto a steel rod so a nut can be threaded onto it. Which hand tool accomplishes this?
choices: Tap
Die
Reamer
Broach
explanation: A die cuts external threads onto a rod or bolt. A tap cuts internal threads inside a hole (to accept a bolt). A reamer smooths and sizes a drilled hole to a precise diameter but does not cut threads.

stem: A star-pattern torque sequence is specified when installing a cylinder head. Why is this sequence used instead of tightening bolts in order from one end to the other?
choices: To finish faster by tightening the center bolts last
To distribute clamping load evenly and prevent the gasket from warping or sealing unevenly
Because cylinder head bolts are left-hand threaded on alternating positions
To allow the bolts to be re-tightened after the engine warms up
explanation: A star or criss-cross pattern pulls the cylinder head down against the gasket from the center outward in stepped increments, so clamping load builds evenly across every bolt and the gasket compresses uniformly. Tightening end-to-end pulls one side down first, which can warp the head or pinch the gasket and leak. Finishing faster is not the point of the sequence. Head bolts are not left-hand threaded on alternating positions. Retorquing after warm-up is required on some older engines but is a separate step, not the reason for the pattern itself.

stem: A feeler gauge (thickness gauge) is used to check the valve clearance in an engine. The technician inserts two blades: a 0.006-inch blade slides with slight drag; a 0.007-inch blade will not enter the gap. What does this tell the technician?
choices: The clearance is exactly 0.006 inches
The clearance is between 0.006 and 0.007 inches
The clearance is less than 0.006 inches
The clearance is greater than 0.007 inches
explanation: A feeler gauge works by go/no-go logic. The 0.006-inch blade fits (gap is at least 0.006 in), but the 0.007-inch blade does not fit (gap is less than 0.007 in). Therefore the clearance lies between 0.006 and 0.007 inches.

stem: In a four-stroke gasoline engine, which stroke draws the air-fuel mixture into the cylinder?
choices: Compression stroke
Power stroke
Intake stroke
Exhaust stroke
explanation: On the intake stroke, the piston travels down the cylinder while the intake valve is open, and the resulting vacuum pulls the air-fuel charge into the combustion chamber. The compression stroke comes next, with both valves closed and the piston moving up to squeeze that charge. The power stroke is when the spark fires and the burning mixture drives the piston back down. The exhaust stroke is the piston moving up with the exhaust valve open to push spent gases out. The order is intake, compression, power, exhaust.

stem: The purpose of a vehicle's clutch in a manual transmission is to:
choices: regulate engine idle speed
temporarily disconnect the engine from the transmission to allow gear changes
convert the alternator's output to DC current
control the flow of coolant to the engine block
explanation: Pressing the clutch pedal pulls the pressure plate away from the friction disc, breaking the link between the engine flywheel and the transmission input shaft so you can move the gear lever without grinding gears against engine speed. Engine idle speed is held by the idle air control or throttle body, not by the clutch. Alternator output is rectified by diodes inside the alternator itself, never by the drivetrain. Coolant flow is gated by the thermostat. Each wrong answer points at a different system unrelated to torque interruption.

stem: What is the function of a vehicle's differential?
choices: It filters fuel before it reaches the injectors
It allows the drive wheels to rotate at different speeds when turning
It regulates the voltage output of the charging system
It compresses refrigerant in the air conditioning system
explanation: When a car rounds a corner, the outer drive wheel travels a longer arc than the inner wheel and has to spin faster, and the differential's bevel gears let the two axles turn at different rates while still splitting engine torque between them. Filtering fuel is the fuel filter's job. Regulating charging voltage is done by the voltage regulator inside the alternator. Compressing refrigerant is the air-conditioning compressor's role. The differential lives in the drive axle, not in the fuel, charging, or HVAC systems.

stem: A vehicle's battery provides current to start the engine. Once the engine is running, which component keeps the battery charged?
choices: Starter motor
Distributor
Alternator
Voltage regulator only
explanation: Once the engine is running, the alternator is belt-driven off the crankshaft and produces the current that recharges the battery and runs every electrical load on the car. The starter motor only spins while the key is in the start position to crank the engine over; after that it disengages. The distributor, on engines that have one, routes spark to plugs and does not generate current. The voltage regulator alone controls how much output the alternator makes, but it does not produce current by itself. The alternator is the generator in this system.

stem: Disc brakes use calipers and rotors. When the brake pedal is pressed, what converts pedal force into hydraulic pressure that actuates the calipers?
choices: The brake booster
The master cylinder
The proportioning valve
The ABS modulator
explanation: The master cylinder is a hydraulic pump tied directly to the brake pedal pushrod. When you press the pedal, a piston inside pushes brake fluid through the steel lines, and that pressure pushes the caliper pistons out against the pads. The brake booster only multiplies the foot force you put into the pedal using engine vacuum; it does not generate hydraulic pressure on its own. The proportioning valve balances front-to-rear pressure, downstream of the master cylinder. The ABS modulator pulses the brakes during a skid but does not create the base pressure.

stem: Engine oil pressure drops to near zero while the engine is running at highway speed. The FIRST action should be to:
choices: Continue driving to the nearest service station
Shut off the engine immediately to prevent catastrophic bearing damage
Add oil while the engine is running
Rev the engine to increase oil pump speed
explanation: Engine oil pressure is what keeps a film of oil between the crankshaft and the bearing surfaces. If pressure drops to near zero, the bearings run dry and metal-on-metal contact spins or seizes them in seconds. Shutting the engine off immediately is the only way to save the engine. Driving on to the next station finishes the damage. Adding oil to a running engine is dangerous near hot moving parts and may not even solve the problem if the issue is the pump or a leak. Revving makes the wear faster, not slower.

stem: An automatic transmission uses a torque converter instead of a clutch. The torque converter:
choices: mechanically locks the engine to the transmission at all times
uses hydraulic fluid to transfer and multiply torque between engine and transmission
shifts gears by means of a centrifugal governor
replaces the differential in front-wheel-drive vehicles
explanation: A torque converter is a sealed fluid coupling. Engine torque spins a pump impeller that throws transmission fluid against a turbine connected to the transmission input shaft, and a stator in the middle redirects fluid flow to multiply torque at low speeds. It does not lock the engine to the transmission solidly except when an internal lockup clutch engages at cruise. Centrifugal governors selected shift points on old automatics but do not transfer torque. The differential is a separate gearset farther down the drivetrain. The torque converter replaces the clutch with fluid, not gears.

stem: A vehicle's temperature gauge climbs into the red zone. Steam is seen coming from under the hood. The MOST likely cause is:
choices: A low transmission fluid level
A burst radiator hose or failed thermostat causing coolant loss
An overcharged battery
A stuck-open EGR valve
explanation: Steam and a rising temperature gauge together indicate coolant is escaping and the engine is no longer being cooled. The most common culprits are a burst hose, blown head gasket, or failed thermostat that traps coolant in the block. Low transmission fluid causes shifting problems, not overheating steam.

stem: A fuel-injected engine uses an oxygen sensor (O2 sensor) in the exhaust stream. The engine control module (ECM) uses the O2 sensor signal to:
choices: determine ignition timing advance based on knock
adjust the fuel injector pulse width to maintain the proper air-fuel ratio
regulate alternator output voltage
control cooling fan speed based on engine temperature
explanation: The oxygen sensor reads how much unburned oxygen is left in the exhaust, which tells the ECM whether the mixture entering the cylinders was rich or lean. The ECM then trims injector pulse width up or down on the next cycle to hold the air-fuel ratio near 14.7 to 1 for catalyst efficiency. Knock-based timing advance comes from the knock sensor, not the O2 sensor. Alternator voltage is governed by the voltage regulator. Cooling fan speed is driven by coolant temperature sensor input. Each wrong answer pairs a different sensor with its own control loop.

stem: A front-wheel-drive vehicle clicks loudly when turning at low speed but is quiet when driving straight. The most likely cause is:
choices: A worn constant-velocity (CV) joint
Low power-steering fluid
A cracked brake rotor
A loose exhaust heat shield
explanation: A constant-velocity joint lets a front drive axle send power through the steering angle as the wheel turns and the suspension moves. When the rubber boot tears, grease escapes, dirt gets in, and the joint's internal balls and races wear, producing a sharp clicking that is loudest on tight, slow turns and quiet when going straight. Low power steering fluid causes a whining or moaning during steering effort, not a click. A cracked brake rotor produces a pulse or grind under braking, not on turns. A loose heat shield rattles with engine RPM, regardless of steering input.

stem: A technician notices that the engine temperature rises quickly to normal then a short time later climbs well above normal, cools slightly, then rises again in a repeating cycle. The MOST likely cause is:
choices: A clogged catalytic converter
A thermostat that is stuck partially open, cycling the engine through warm-cool oscillations
A blown head gasket allowing combustion gases into the cooling system
A faulty coolant temperature sensor sending erratic signals to the ECM
explanation: A blown head gasket allows combustion gases (under high pressure) to enter the cooling system. These gases displace coolant, form pockets that reduce cooling efficiency, and can cause the temperature to cycle as coolant is intermittently lost and returned. A stuck thermostat causes persistent overheating or chronic under-temperature, not cycling. A faulty sensor changes gauge readings but not actual temperature.

stem: An engine knocks heavily under load but the knock nearly disappears at idle. This is most consistent with:
choices: A cracked flywheel
Pre-ignition (detonation) caused by low-octane fuel or advanced timing
A broken engine mount
An air leak in the intake manifold
explanation: Detonation, also called spark knock or ping, happens when cylinder pressure and heat are high enough that part of the air-fuel charge ignites on its own before the spark plug fires, slamming a shock wave into the piston. Heavy load is exactly when pressure and temperature peak, which is why the knock fades at idle. Common causes are gasoline with octane below what the engine needs, carbon deposits glowing hot in the chamber, or timing advanced too far. A cracked flywheel makes a noise tied to RPM, not load. A broken motor mount thumps on shifts. An intake leak shows up as a lean miss and rough idle, the opposite load pattern.

stem: When working in a shop where gasoline or solvent vapors may be present, adequate ventilation is required primarily to:
choices: keep tools at the correct operating temperature
prevent accumulation of flammable vapors that could ignite
ensure that paint dries faster
reduce humidity that causes rust on tools
explanation: Gasoline and most solvent vapors are denser than air, so they pool at floor level in a closed shop and can build up to a concentration that ignites from a spark, a pilot light, or a piece of running equipment. Moving air through the space keeps the concentration below the lower explosive limit. Tool operating temperature is not driven by shop ventilation. Paint drying is a side benefit on some jobs but not the safety reason for the rule. Rust prevention is about humidity control, which is a different problem with a different solution. Ventilation is fundamentally about explosion and fire prevention.

stem: A small Class B fire breaks out in the shop. Class B fires involve flammable liquids such as gasoline or oil. Which type of extinguisher is appropriate?
choices: Water extinguisher
Class A dry extinguisher
CO2 or dry chemical extinguisher rated for Class B
Any available extinguisher regardless of rating
explanation: Class B fires involve flammable liquids like gasoline, oil, and solvents, and the right answer is a CO2 or dry chemical extinguisher rated for Class B. CO2 smothers the fire by displacing oxygen, and dry chemical interrupts the chemical chain reaction. Water spreads burning liquid because the fuel floats on top of the water and rides the splash. A Class A only extinguisher, designed for wood and paper, will not safely handle a fuel fire. Grabbing any extinguisher regardless of rating risks making the situation worse. Match the extinguisher class to the fuel class.

stem: Lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures are required before performing maintenance on a machine. The PRIMARY purpose of LOTO is to:
choices: document the maintenance procedure for quality records
prevent the machine from being energized while a worker is inside or working on it
notify supervisors that the equipment is being serviced
ensure tools are returned to their correct storage locations
explanation: Lockout/tagout puts a physical lock on the energy isolation point, a breaker, valve, or disconnect, so the equipment cannot be powered up while a worker has hands or body inside it. The lock and tag stay there until the worker who applied them removes them. Documenting maintenance is a paperwork goal, not a safety control. Notifying supervisors is communication, not energy isolation. Returning tools to storage is housekeeping. Only physical lockout actually prevents the startup that injures or kills the technician.

stem: A shop worker spills a small amount of motor oil on the floor. The safest IMMEDIATE action is to:
choices: Mark the spill with a cone and clean it up at the end of the shift
Spread sawdust or absorbent material and clean it up promptly
Wipe it with a rag and place the rag in an open trash can
Pour water over the spill to dilute it
explanation: An oil puddle is a slip hazard the instant it hits the floor, so the safest move is to spread oil-absorbent material or sawdust, sweep it up, and dispose of it properly right away. Marking the spill with a cone and waiting until end of shift leaves a fall hazard live for hours. Wiping with a rag and tossing it in an open trash can risks spontaneous combustion as the oil oxidizes inside a pile of paper trash. Pouring water spreads the oil and does not remove it, and the floor stays slick. Immediate absorbent cleanup is the standard.

stem: A worker is grinding metal and is producing sparks and metal fragments. Besides eye protection, what additional PPE should be worn?
choices: Latex examination gloves and a dust mask
Face shield over safety glasses, leather gloves, and hearing protection
Safety glasses only, sparks are not hazardous at short range
Chemical-splash goggles and a rubber apron
explanation: Grinding throws hot sparks, sharp metal fragments, and abrasive grit, and the wheel screams loud enough to damage hearing on a long job, so the worker needs a face shield over safety glasses for the face, leather gloves for hot edges, and hearing protection for the noise. Latex exam gloves and a paper dust mask offer no impact, heat, or noise protection. Safety glasses alone leave the rest of the face exposed to flying debris and slag. Chemical-splash goggles and a rubber apron are dressed for solvent or acid work, not for incandescent sparks that would burn through rubber.

stem: Before lifting a vehicle with a floor jack to work underneath it, you MUST also:
choices: Remove the wheels first to reduce vehicle weight
Place jack stands under the vehicle's frame or pinch-weld points to support it
Set the parking brake and leave the floor jack as the only support
Run the engine for 10 minutes to warm the oil before working
explanation: A hydraulic floor jack lifts the vehicle, but a seal can leak or a valve can release, and the jack can drop. Jack stands are rigid steel supports that mechanically hold the load while you work, so you place them under the frame rails or pinch welds before crawling under. Removing the wheels does not address the falling-vehicle hazard. The parking brake on its own, with the jack as sole support, ignores the failure mode that kills people. Warming up the oil is unrelated to whether the car is safely supported. Always pair the jack with stands before going underneath.

stem: Oily rags used for wiping up solvent or paint should be stored in a:
choices: Cardboard box kept away from windows
Sealed metal container approved for flammable waste
Plastic bin lined with a trash bag
Open drum to allow vapors to escape
explanation: Oils and many solvents react slowly with oxygen and give off heat, and rags piled together insulate that heat until they reach the ignition point on their own, a process called spontaneous combustion. A sealed metal can starves any fire of oxygen and contains the heat away from the rest of the shop. Cardboard burns through in minutes once a fire starts inside it. Plastic bins soften and melt and do not contain a fire at all. An open drum lets vapors escape into the air and feeds any spark that finds them. Metal, sealed, and approved is the standard.

stem: A worker is about to use a bench grinder and notices that the grinding wheel has a small crack running from the center outward. The correct action is to:
choices: Run it at low speed only to avoid spreading the crack
Replace the wheel immediately; a cracked wheel can shatter at high RPM
Apply epoxy to the crack and allow it to cure before use
Use the wheel on the side face rather than the rim to avoid the crack
explanation: A cracked abrasive wheel can explode at operating RPM, sending fragments outward with great force. Any crack, chip, or damage is grounds for immediate replacement. Running it at lower speed or using the side face does not eliminate the risk of sudden catastrophic failure.

stem: Which class of fire involves energized electrical equipment?
choices: Class A
Class B
Class C
Class D
explanation: Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment such as panels, motors, or wiring, and the rule is to use a non-conductive extinguisher like CO2 or dry chemical so you do not get shocked through the stream. Class A is ordinary combustibles like wood, paper, and cloth. Class B is flammable liquids such as gasoline, oil, and solvents. Class D is combustible metals like magnesium and sodium. Memorizing the letter-to-fuel mapping is the only way to choose the right extinguisher quickly under stress.

stem: Before entering a large tank or enclosed space for maintenance work, a technician should first:
choices: Enter quickly and complete the task before vapors build up
Test the atmosphere for oxygen level and toxic or flammable vapors
Bring a lit lamp inside to check for visibility
Seal any ventilation openings to keep temperature stable
explanation: A tank or other confined space can hold an oxygen-deficient atmosphere, leftover toxic vapors, or flammable residues, and you cannot see or smell most of those hazards. A calibrated four-gas meter has to test oxygen, combustibles, and toxic gases before anyone goes in. Entering quickly to beat the buildup ignores that the hazardous atmosphere may already be there. A lit lamp inside an unknown atmosphere is an ignition source. Sealing ventilation makes the atmosphere worse, not safer. Test, ventilate, and only then enter, with a permit.

stem: A shop receives a drum of a new cleaning solvent. The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) lists an IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health) concentration of 100 ppm. This value means that:
choices: 100 ppm is the safe, permissible exposure limit for an 8-hour shift
at 100 ppm, a worker without a respirator faces conditions from which escape may be impaired or irreversible harm may occur
100 ppm is the concentration at which the solvent becomes flammable
100 ppm must be measured monthly to comply with OSHA standards
explanation: IDLH is the concentration at which a worker who loses respiratory protection has roughly thirty minutes to get out before symptoms keep them from escaping or before irreversible health damage sets in. It is an emergency-planning value used to pick respirator type for entry or rescue work. The permissible exposure limit, PEL, is the much lower routine-exposure ceiling for an eight-hour shift, so the safe-shift answer is wrong. IDLH says nothing directly about flammability, which is set by the lower explosive limit. The monthly-monitoring rule is invented; OSHA monitoring frequency depends on the specific substance.

stem: A shop policy requires that all rotating machinery guards be in place before the machine is started. A supervisor removes a guard temporarily for an adjustment and then leaves without replacing it. The correct action for the next technician who needs to use the machine is to:
choices: Use the machine quickly and then replace the guard afterward
Report the missing guard and refuse to operate the machine until the guard is reinstalled
Operate the machine while holding the guard in place manually
Tape cardboard over the exposed area as a temporary barrier
explanation: Machine guards keep hands and clothing out of rotating gears, belts, and pinch points, and they are a primary engineering control required before the machine runs. The right move is to stop, report the missing guard, and refuse to operate until it is replaced. Running it briefly without the guard exposes the worker for exactly the time the hazard is loose. Holding the guard by hand puts the holding hand inside the danger zone. Cardboard taped over the opening is not a guard, will not stop a rotating part, and can be pulled in. The line is clear: no guard, no operation.

stem: Coolant reservoir level is checked and found to be below the MIN mark while the engine is cold. What is the correct action?
choices: Add the correct antifreeze-water mixture to bring the level to the MIN-MAX range
Drain and replace all coolant regardless of level
Add straight water only so coolant does not become too concentrated
The reservoir level is irrelevant; check only the radiator cap
explanation: On a modern engine the overflow reservoir is where you check and add coolant when the engine is cold, and the right top-up is the manufacturer-specified antifreeze and water mix, typically a 50/50 blend, to keep both freeze and boil protection intact. Draining and replacing all the coolant is a full service, not a level correction. Adding straight water dilutes the antifreeze and weakens corrosion inhibitors. The radiator cap is not a service point for level on systems built around a reservoir, and opening a hot cap is a burn risk anyway. Add the correct mix, to the correct mark, when cold.

stem: The low-oil pressure warning light illuminates while idling at a stoplight. The engine sounds normal. The driver should FIRST:
choices: Rev the engine to 3,000 RPM to build oil pressure
Turn off the engine, check the oil level, and add oil if needed before restarting
Continue driving, oil pressure lights are often faulty
Add transmission fluid to the engine to restore pressure quickly
explanation: A low oil pressure warning at idle can mean the level is genuinely low, the pump or bearings are failing, or the sensor itself is bad, and you cannot tell which one without stopping. Shutting the engine off protects the bearings from oil starvation, then a dipstick check shows whether oil is needed before restart. Revving the engine pushes more oil only at the cost of more wear if pressure really is gone. Driving on because the light might be faulty is exactly the gamble that destroys engines. Transmission fluid is not engine oil and will damage seals and clutches that do not belong in an engine.

stem: A technician checks the brake fluid and finds it is dark brown and appears contaminated. The correct service action is to:
choices: Top off with fresh fluid to dilute the contamination
Flush the brake system and fill with fresh fluid of the correct DOT specification
Add a brake fluid conditioner and continue use
No action needed, brake fluid darkens naturally and remains effective
explanation: Brake fluid is hygroscopic, meaning it pulls moisture out of the air over time, and that water lowers the boiling point and corrodes calipers, lines, and the master cylinder from the inside. Dark brown fluid is contaminated and needs to be flushed completely from each wheel and refilled with fresh fluid matching the vehicle's DOT specification. Topping off only mixes new fluid with bad and leaves the contamination in place. Brake fluid conditioners are not a recognized service for this and cannot undo moisture absorption. The claim that dark fluid is normal and effective is simply wrong; brake fluid should be light amber.

stem: A driver notices the engine hesitates and stumbles when accelerating from a stop. A mechanic inspects and finds the air filter is completely clogged with dirt. Why would a clogged air filter cause this symptom?
choices: The filter restricts airflow, creating an excessively rich fuel mixture that makes the engine stumble under load
The filter blocks cooling airflow to the radiator
A clogged filter increases intake air temperature, advancing ignition timing
The filter restricts oil return to the crankcase
explanation: The engine needs a precise air-fuel ratio. A severely clogged air filter restricts incoming air, causing the mixture to become too rich (too much fuel relative to air). Under load the engine stumbles because combustion is incomplete. At idle, the effect may be minor, but under acceleration it becomes pronounced.

stem: A technician replaces a worn serpentine belt. After installation, the belt squeals briefly when the engine is cold but goes away when warmed. The MOST likely cause is:
choices: The replacement belt is too long
Slight slipping on a pulley due to condensation or belt material until the belt seats fully
The tensioner pulley bearing has failed
A mismatch in belt rib count
explanation: Brief cold squealing from a new belt is common as the rubber conforms to pulley grooves and surface moisture evaporates. If squealing persists or occurs warm, it indicates a worn tensioner, glazed pulley, or incorrect belt. Rib-count mismatches produce constant noise and would cause the belt to mistrack.

stem: When replacing brake pads on a disc brake vehicle, a technician compresses the caliper piston back into the caliper bore. Before doing so, what precaution should be taken?
choices: Open the bleeder screw to release pressure while compressing the piston
Remove the master cylinder reservoir cap and siphon out some fluid to prevent overflow as the piston is pushed back
Disconnect the ABS module to prevent error codes
Add brake fluid to the reservoir to prevent air from entering
explanation: Pushing the caliper piston back forces fluid backward into the master cylinder reservoir. If the reservoir is full, this fluid has nowhere to go and will overflow, potentially damaging painted surfaces or rubber seals. Removing the reservoir cap and siphoning excess fluid first prevents overflow. (The bleeder screw method also works but requires catching old fluid separately.)

stem: A spark plug removed from a cylinder has a wet, oily black deposit coating the ceramic insulator and electrodes. This most likely indicates:
choices: The engine is running too lean
Oil is entering the combustion chamber due to worn piston rings or valve seals
The ignition timing is excessively advanced
The coolant system is leaking into the cylinder
explanation: A wet, oily, black coating on the plug means liquid oil is reaching the combustion chamber, either past worn piston rings from below or past failed valve stem seals from above, and it is being burned along with the fuel. A lean mixture, with too little fuel, leaves a chalky white or very light gray deposit because there is no extra fuel to leave soot. Excessively advanced ignition timing produces detonation symptoms and may erode the electrode, not coat it with oil. A coolant leak into the cylinder bakes onto the plug as a hard white crust, sometimes with a sweet smell, not a wet black film.

stem: A vehicle's power steering pump whines loudly when the steering wheel is turned to full lock in either direction. The fluid level is within the normal range. The MOST likely cause is:
choices: Overfilled power steering reservoir
Normal cavitation at the pressure limit, acceptable as long as not held at full lock for extended time
A failing electric power steering motor
A clogged hydraulic return line causing system overpressure
explanation: Hydraulic power steering systems reach their maximum pressure limit at full steering lock. The pump continues to build pressure against a closed circuit, causing fluid to cavitate (form vapor bubbles) and produce a whining or moaning sound. This is normal for short periods; holding full lock for extended time can damage the pump. If it whines at all steering positions, that indicates a different problem.

stem: A visual inspection of a vehicle's exhaust smoke reveals continuous white smoke from a cold engine that does NOT clear after warm-up. This most likely indicates:
choices: Normal condensation burning off
A rich fuel mixture from a faulty injector
Coolant entering the combustion chamber through a failed head gasket
Burning engine oil due to a cracked piston
explanation: Light white smoke on cold startup that clears quickly is normal condensation. Persistent white smoke after warm-up that has a sweet smell and does not dissipate strongly indicates coolant (antifreeze) burning in the cylinders, a hallmark of a blown head gasket or cracked block. Oil smoke tends to be blue-gray with an acrid odor.

stem: A technician performs a cylinder compression test and finds cylinder 3 has 80 psi while all others read 165 psi. Adding a small amount of oil to cylinder 3 through the spark plug hole raises its compression to 150 psi. What does this indicate?
choices: A bent intake valve in cylinder 3
Worn piston rings in cylinder 3, the oil temporarily seals the gap
A cracked cylinder wall in cylinder 3
A leaking head gasket between cylinders 3 and 4
explanation: This is the wet compression test. Adding oil temporarily seals the ring-to-cylinder-wall gap. If compression rises significantly when oil is added, the low reading is caused by ring wear or blow-by, not by a valve or gasket problem. A valve issue would not improve with oil because valves seal above the rings.

stem: After replacing a vehicle's oxygen sensor, the technician clears the fault code and performs a drive cycle, but the check-engine light returns with the same code. The NEXT diagnostic step is to:
choices: Replace the oxygen sensor again with a higher-grade part
Check wiring harness continuity and inspect for exhaust leaks upstream of the sensor
Replace the catalytic converter, as it is the usual cause of sensor codes
Reprogram the ECM with updated software
explanation: If a new sensor triggers the same code, the sensor is likely not the root cause. The technician should inspect the wiring harness for shorts, opens, or damaged connectors, and check for exhaust leaks upstream that can introduce false oxygen readings. Replacing parts without checking the circuit is inefficient and costly.

stem: A technician finds the battery terminals heavily corroded with a white-blue crusty buildup. After cleaning the terminals, what is the correct follow-up action?
choices: Replace the battery immediately regardless of its test results
Apply a battery terminal protector spray or grease to prevent future corrosion
Coat the terminals with motor oil for corrosion protection
Tighten the terminal clamps as hard as possible to ensure good contact
explanation: After cleaning corrosion from battery terminals, a protective spray (petroleum-based terminal spray or anti-corrosion felt pads) applied to the terminals significantly reduces the rate of future corrosion by sealing the metal from battery acid vapors. Motor oil is not intended for this purpose and can attract dirt.

stem: Which class of lever has the effort force applied between the fulcrum and the load?
choices: First-class lever
Second-class lever
Third-class lever
Fourth-class lever
explanation: A third-class lever places the effort between the fulcrum and the load. Common examples include tweezers, fishing rods, and the human forearm lifting a weight. Choice A (first-class) places the fulcrum between effort and load, like a seesaw or pliers. Choice B (second-class) places the load between fulcrum and effort, like a wheelbarrow or bottle opener. Choice D (fourth-class) does not exist in mechanics; only three classes of lever are defined, distinguished by which of the three elements (fulcrum, load, effort) sits in the middle.

stem: A first-class lever has its fulcrum exactly in the middle, with a 20-pound weight on each side. The lever is:
choices: Balanced, with no mechanical advantage
Tipped toward the left side
Tipped toward the right side
Providing a mechanical advantage of 2
explanation: A lever balances when torques are equal: W1 x D1 = W2 x D2. With 20 pounds at equal distances, both torques match and the lever is balanced. Mechanical advantage = effort arm / load arm = 1, so no force is multiplied. Choice B (tipped left) and choice C (tipped right) ignore that the moments are equal. Choice D (MA of 2) requires the effort arm to be twice the load arm; here they are equal. A first-class lever with arms of equal length only redirects force, much like a fixed pulley.

stem: A lever has a 4-foot effort arm and a 1-foot load arm. What is the mechanical advantage?
explanation: Lever MA equals effort arm divided by load arm: MA = 4 / 1 = 4. This means 1 pound of effort can lift a 4-pound load (ignoring the lever's own weight). Choice A (1) would require equal arm lengths and corresponds to a balanced first-class lever with no mechanical advantage. Choice B (2) halves the correct ratio. Choice D (5) adds the arm lengths (4 + 1) instead of dividing them. Mechanical advantage is always a ratio of effort arm to load arm, never a sum.

stem: A movable pulley is attached to the load rather than to a fixed point. Compared to a single fixed pulley, a single movable pulley:
choices: Provides no change in mechanical advantage
Doubles the mechanical advantage to 2 by supporting the load with two rope segments
Triples the mechanical advantage to 3
Reverses the direction of pull with no force benefit
explanation: A single movable pulley is attached to the load and supported by two rope segments. Each segment shares half the load, so the effort needed is half the load weight; MA = 2. The trade-off is that the effort end of the rope must travel twice as far as the load rises (conservation of work). Choice A (no change) describes a fixed pulley, not a movable one. Choice C (triples to 3) requires three supporting strands, which a single movable pulley does not have. Choice D (reverses direction with no force benefit) describes a fixed pulley's behavior, not a movable one.

stem: A 90-pound load is placed 2 feet from the fulcrum of a lever. To balance the load, how much force must be applied at a point 6 feet from the fulcrum on the other side?
choices: 15 pounds
30 pounds
45 pounds
180 pounds
explanation: Lever equilibrium: Load × Load arm = Effort × Effort arm. 90 × 2 = Effort × 6. Effort = 180 / 6 = 30 pounds. The common trap is dividing the load by the load arm (90 / 2 = 45) rather than setting up the full balance equation.

stem: A compound pulley system has one fixed pulley and two movable pulleys, resulting in 5 rope segments supporting the load. If the load weighs 200 pounds, how much effort force is needed (ignoring friction)?
choices: 20 pounds
40 pounds
50 pounds
100 pounds
explanation: Mechanical advantage = number of rope segments supporting the load = 5. Effort = Load / MA = 200 / 5 = 40 pounds. Dividing by the number of pulleys rather than rope segments is the common error.

stem: A crowbar (first-class lever) is 4 feet long. The fulcrum is placed 6 inches from the load end. How long is the effort arm?
choices: 6 inches
42 inches
3.5 feet
4 feet
explanation: Total lever length = 4 feet = 48 inches. Load arm = 6 inches. Effort arm = 48 − 6 = 42 inches. MA = 42 / 6 = 7, a significant mechanical advantage. The trap is reporting the effort arm as 4 feet (the whole lever).

stem: A flagpole uses a fixed pulley at the top. When you pull the rope down, the flag goes up. What does this pulley change?
choices: The magnitude of the required force
Only the direction of the applied force
Both the direction and the magnitude of the force
The speed of the rope only
explanation: A single fixed pulley has a mechanical advantage of 1, it does not reduce the force needed. Its only benefit is redirecting the force: you pull down to move the load up, which is more convenient. To reduce required force you need a movable pulley or a compound system.

stem: A block-and-tackle system supports a 360-pound load with 6 rope segments. You pull the free rope end upward 12 feet. How far does the load actually rise?
choices: 2 feet
6 feet
12 feet
72 feet
explanation: With 6 rope segments sharing the load, you trade force for distance: MA = 6. Load distance = rope distance / MA = 12 / 6 = 2 feet. You exert 360 / 6 = 60 pounds of effort but must pull 6 feet of rope for each foot the load moves.

stem: A wheelbarrow is a second-class lever. The wheel axle acts as the fulcrum; the load sits in the center; the handles are gripped at the far end. If the load weighs 150 pounds and sits 1.5 feet from the axle, and the handles are 4.5 feet from the axle, how much upward force at the handles is needed to lift the load?
choices: 50 pounds
75 pounds
100 pounds
225 pounds
explanation: Torque balance: Effort x Effort arm = Load x Load arm. So Effort x 4.5 = 150 x 1.5 = 225 lb-ft. Effort = 225 / 4.5 = 50 pounds. Choice B (75 pounds) is half the load weight, the answer for a single movable pulley, not a second-class lever with these arm lengths. Choice C (100 pounds) ignores the load arm and divides 150 by some other ratio. Choice D (225 pounds) is the torque value (in lb-ft) being misread as a force. MA = effort arm / load arm = 4.5 / 1.5 = 3, so effort is one-third of load weight.

stem: Two pulleys are connected by a belt. The driving pulley has a diameter of 8 inches and turns at 300 RPM. The driven pulley has a diameter of 4 inches. At what RPM does the driven pulley spin?
choices: 150 RPM
300 RPM
600 RPM
1200 RPM
explanation: Belt-connected pulleys have rim speeds equal (no slip), so RPM is inversely proportional to diameter: Driven RPM = Driver RPM x (Driver d / Driven d) = 300 x (8 / 4) = 600 RPM. Choice A (150 RPM) inverts the ratio and treats the larger pulley as the faster one. Choice B (300 RPM) leaves the speed unchanged, ignoring the size difference. Choice D (1200 RPM) doubles the correct answer, possibly multiplying by 4 instead of by 2. The smaller driven pulley always spins faster than the larger driver when belt-coupled.

stem: A mechanic uses a breaker bar that is 24 inches long to loosen a bolt. She can apply 60 pounds of force. The bolt requires 180 pound-inches of torque to loosen. Can she loosen it, and if not, what minimum bar length is needed?
choices: Yes, 24 inches is sufficient
No; she needs at least 3 inches
No; she needs at least 30 inches
No; she needs at least 36 inches
explanation: Torque = Force × Arm length = 60 × 24 = 1,440 pound-inches. The bolt requires only 180 pound-inches, so 24 inches is more than sufficient. The trap is misreading the question; 60 × 24 = 1,440, which greatly exceeds 180.

stem: A lever system is set up with the fulcrum 1 foot from the load end and 5 feet from the effort end (total 6 feet). A 200-pound load is placed at the load end. How much effort is needed, and what is the direction of effort relative to the load for a first-class lever?
choices: 40 pounds applied upward, same direction as the load pushes
40 pounds applied downward, opposite direction to the load
200 pounds applied downward
40 pounds applied horizontally
explanation: Effort = Load × Load arm / Effort arm = 200 × 1 / 5 = 40 pounds. In a first-class lever the fulcrum is between effort and load, so effort and load forces act in opposite directions, pressing down at the effort end lifts the load end up. The effort is applied downward (opposite to the upward force the load exerts on its end).

stem: Two meshing spur gears always rotate in:
choices: the same direction
opposite directions
alternating directions depending on speed
the same direction only when they are the same size
explanation: When two external spur gears mesh, the teeth push against each other at the contact point. Equal and opposite contact forces (Newton's Third Law) cause the two gears to spin in opposite directions. Choice A (same direction) only happens with an idler gear between them, or with internal gears. Choice C (alternating depending on speed) is not how gear direction works; direction is set by geometry, not speed. Choice D (same direction when equal size) is also wrong; same-size meshing gears still rotate opposite each other. To get same direction without size change, an idler gear must be inserted.

stem: A driving gear has 8 teeth and the driven gear has 24 teeth. The gear ratio (driven:driver) is:
explanation: Gear ratio (driven to driver) equals teeth on driven divided by teeth on driver: 24 / 8 = 3:1. The driving gear turns three times for every one turn of the driven gear, producing a 3:1 speed reduction and a 3:1 torque multiplication. Choice A (1:3) inverts the ratio, which would describe a speed-up gear set (small driver, larger driven). Choice C (8:1) and choice D (1:8) use the wrong tooth count or completely skip the division step. Direction of the ratio (driven:driver vs driver:driven) must always be matched to context; the convention here is driven first.

stem: Gear A has 12 teeth and turns at 600 RPM. It meshes with Gear B, which has 36 teeth. How fast does Gear B rotate?
choices: 100 RPM
200 RPM
300 RPM
explanation: RPM is inversely proportional to tooth count: RPM_B = RPM_A x (Teeth_A / Teeth_B) = 600 x (12 / 36) = 600 x (1/3) = 200 RPM. Choice A (100 RPM) divides by 6 instead of 3, doubling the reduction. Choice C (300 RPM) only halves the speed, applying a 2:1 ratio. Choice D (1,800 RPM) multiplies by 3 instead of dividing, treating the larger gear as the faster one. The bigger gear always rotates slower than the smaller gear it meshes with; torque trades up by the same factor speed trades down.

stem: In a gear train, decreasing rotational speed by using a larger driven gear also has what effect on torque?
choices: Torque decreases proportionally
Torque increases proportionally
Torque is unaffected by the gear ratio
Torque doubles regardless of the ratio
explanation: Gears trade speed for torque (assuming no friction losses). When the driven gear is larger, it turns slower but the torque multiplies by the same ratio. If speed is halved, torque is doubled. This is why low gears in a vehicle provide more pulling force.

stem: Three gears are in a row: Gear 1 (driver, 10 teeth) meshes with Gear 2 (20 teeth, idler), which meshes with Gear 3 (10 teeth). In which direction does Gear 3 turn relative to Gear 1?
choices: Opposite direction, the idler reverses the direction once
Same direction, each mesh reverses direction, and two reversals return to original
Gear 3 does not rotate when an idler is present
Gear 3 rotates 90 degrees out of phase with Gear 1
explanation: Each external gear mesh reverses the direction of rotation. Gear 1 to Gear 2 is one reversal; Gear 2 to Gear 3 is a second reversal. Two reversals return Gear 3 to the same rotational direction as Gear 1. The idler also makes the final speed ratio depend only on the first and last gears (10 and 10), not the idler in the middle, so the speed ratio is 1:1. Choice A (opposite) counts only one reversal. Choice C (does not rotate) misunderstands what an idler does; it transmits motion. Choice D (90 degrees out of phase) confuses rotational direction with phase angle, which is not a quantity defined for spur gears.

stem: A wheel and axle system has a wheel radius of 15 cm and an axle radius of 3 cm. A 60-Newton load is hung from the axle. What force must be applied at the rim of the wheel to hold the load in equilibrium?
choices: 4 N
explanation: MA of a wheel and axle = wheel radius / axle radius = 15 / 3 = 5. Effort = Load / MA = 60 / 5 = 12 N. A common trap is dividing 60 by 15 (the wheel radius alone) to get 4 N, confusing radius with MA.

stem: A gear reduction system uses a 5-tooth pinion driving a 40-tooth ring gear. The input shaft turns at 2,000 RPM. What is the output RPM, and how does the output torque compare to the input?
choices: 250 RPM output; torque is multiplied by 8
250 RPM output; torque is the same
16,000 RPM output; torque is divided by 8
400 RPM output; torque is multiplied by 5
explanation: Output RPM = Input RPM x (Pinion teeth / Ring teeth) = 2,000 x (5/40) = 250 RPM. The gear reduction ratio is 40/5 = 8:1. In an ideal gear set, what is lost in speed is gained in torque, so output torque = input torque x 8. Choice B (250 RPM, same torque) gets the speed right but ignores the speed-torque trade-off. Choice C (16,000 RPM, torque /8) inverts the formula and treats the small gear as the driven side. Choice D (400 RPM, torque x 5) uses the wrong ratio (5 instead of 8). Gear ratio always equals teeth_driven / teeth_driver.

stem: A bicycle chainring has 48 teeth and the rear sprocket has 16 teeth. For each complete revolution of the pedals, how many times does the rear wheel turn?
choices: 1 time
2 times
3 times
4 times
explanation: Chain drives behave like gears: rear wheel revolutions per pedal revolution = chainring teeth / sprocket teeth = 48 / 16 = 3. Each pedal stroke turns the rear wheel three times, giving a speed advantage at the cost of higher pedaling force. Choice A (1 time) ignores the tooth ratio entirely. Choice B (2 times) halves the correct ratio. Choice D (4 times) uses the wrong division (perhaps 48 / 12 or similar). Larger chainring relative to sprocket means more wheel revolutions per pedal turn, which is the trade-off cyclists make for higher top speed.

stem: In a planetary gear set, the sun gear has 20 teeth, each of three planet gears has 10 teeth, and the ring gear has 40 teeth. If the ring gear is held fixed and power is applied to the sun gear, which output produces the highest torque?
choices: The planet carrier (output through the arm holding the planets)
The ring gear (despite being held)
The sun gear itself
All outputs produce identical torque
explanation: When the ring gear is fixed and the sun drives the system, the planet gears walk around the fixed ring, carrying the planet carrier (arm) as output. The speed reduction is significant (overall ratio = 1 + Ring/Sun = 1 + 40/20 = 3:1), giving 3× torque multiplication at the carrier, the highest available output torque configuration for this setup.

stem: A worm gear pair has a worm with 1 thread (start) and a worm wheel with 50 teeth. If the worm shaft turns at 1,500 RPM, at what speed does the worm wheel turn?
choices: 1 RPM
30 RPM
50 RPM
explanation: A single-start worm advances the worm wheel by one tooth per full worm revolution. With 50 teeth on the wheel, the gear ratio is 50:1. Output RPM = Input RPM / 50 = 1,500 / 50 = 30 RPM. Choice A (1 RPM) over-reduces, dividing by 1,500 or otherwise inflating the ratio. Choice C (50 RPM) leaves out one step in the division. Choice D (75,000 RPM) multiplies instead of dividing, treating the worm wheel as a speed-up element. Worm gears are extreme speed reducers and large torque multipliers, often used where high torque at low speed is needed.

stem: A 4-speed manual transmission has gear ratios of 3.5:1, 2.2:1, 1.4:1, and 1:1 for first through fourth gear. In first gear with the engine producing 200 Newton-meters of torque, approximately how much torque is available at the transmission output shaft (ignoring drivetrain losses)?
choices: 57 Nm
200 Nm
440 Nm
700 Nm
explanation: Gear reduction multiplies torque: Output torque = Engine torque x Gear ratio = 200 Nm x 3.5 = 700 Nm. Choice A (57 Nm) divides 200 by 3.5, inverting the formula and treating first gear as a speed-up. Choice B (200 Nm) ignores the gear ratio and just lists engine torque, which would only be the case in fourth gear (1:1). Choice C (440 Nm) uses the 2.2:1 second-gear ratio instead of first gear. First gear has the highest reduction and therefore the highest output torque, which is exactly why vehicles start from rest in first gear.

stem: Two meshing gears have a center-to-center distance of 18 cm. Gear A (the driver) has a pitch radius of 6 cm. Expressing gear ratio as driven-to-driver, what is the gear ratio?
explanation: For meshing external gears the center-to-center distance equals the sum of the two pitch radii. So radius_B = 18 minus 6 = 12 cm. Pitch radius is proportional to tooth count, so the driven-to-driver ratio is 12 to 6, which simplifies to 2:1. The driven gear (B) is twice as large, so for every one rotation of the driver it turns half a revolution, and its torque is doubled. '1:1' would require equal radii (9 and 9). '1:2' inverts the convention and would describe driver-to-driven instead. '3:1' would require radius_B = 18, which violates the geometric constraint.

stem: A rack and pinion gear system converts which type of motion to which?
choices: Rotary motion to linear motion
Linear motion to circular oscillation
Vertical motion to horizontal rotary motion
Torque to electrical energy
explanation: A rack is a straight, flat gear; a pinion is a circular gear. When the pinion rotates, its teeth engage the rack and push it along its length, converting rotary motion into linear motion. This is the operating principle of rack-and-pinion steering, where steering-wheel rotation becomes linear motion of the tie rods. Choice B (linear to circular oscillation) reverses the input/output and adds oscillation, which is not what a rack-and-pinion does. Choice C (vertical to horizontal rotary) garbles the geometry; the conversion is rotary-to-linear, not vertical-to-horizontal. Choice D (torque to electricity) describes a generator, not a mechanical gear set.

stem: A 50-pound box sits on a rough surface. A horizontal force of 20 pounds is applied but the box does not move. Which statement correctly describes the friction force acting on the box?
choices: Friction is 50 pounds, opposing the weight of the box
Friction is 20 pounds, exactly opposing the applied force
Friction is zero because the box is not moving
Friction is 70 pounds, combining weight and applied force
explanation: Static friction adjusts to exactly cancel the applied horizontal force when the object is not moving. The box is in equilibrium, so the horizontal forces must balance: static friction = 20 pounds directed opposite to the applied force.

stem: Compared to static friction, kinetic (sliding) friction for the same object on the same surface is generally:
choices: Greater
Equal
Less
Dependent only on velocity
explanation: Kinetic (sliding) friction is generally less than the maximum static friction for the same surfaces. Once an object breaks free of static friction and begins sliding, fewer microscopic surface interlocks form, so the resisting force drops. Choice A (greater) reverses the well-established relationship. Choice B (equal) ignores the difference between coefficients of static and kinetic friction. Choice D (depends only on velocity) is wrong; for most practical surfaces, kinetic friction is approximately constant with velocity at typical speeds. This is why a heavy object takes more force to start moving than to keep moving.

stem: A 100-pound block rests on a horizontal surface with a coefficient of static friction of 0.4. What minimum horizontal force is needed to start the block moving?
choices: 20 pounds
40 pounds
60 pounds
100 pounds
explanation: Maximum static friction equals the coefficient of static friction times the normal force: f_s = mu_s x N = 0.4 x 100 = 40 pounds. On a horizontal surface, normal force equals the object's weight, so 40 pounds of push is the minimum to start motion. Choice A (20 pounds) halves the coefficient or uses 0.2 by mistake. Choice C (60 pounds) inflates the coefficient to 0.6. Choice D (100 pounds) ignores the coefficient and uses the full weight as the friction force, which would only be true if mu_s equaled 1.0.

stem: A 200-pound box is being dragged across a floor. The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.25. What is the friction force resisting the motion?
choices: 25 pounds
50 pounds
80 pounds
200 pounds
explanation: Kinetic friction equals the coefficient times the normal force: f_k = mu_k x N = 0.25 x 200 = 50 pounds. On a flat floor the normal force equals the box's weight. Choice A (25 pounds) halves the coefficient to 0.125. Choice C (80 pounds) uses a coefficient of 0.4 instead of 0.25. Choice D (200 pounds) ignores the coefficient and uses the full weight, which would only apply if mu_k were 1.0 (an extreme value rarely seen). Friction always scales with normal force, not with the applied force.

stem: A 60-pound block is placed on a ramp inclined at 30 degrees. The normal force on the block from the ramp surface equals the component of the block's weight perpendicular to the ramp surface. If the ramp is smooth (frictionless), the normal force is approximately:
choices: 30 pounds
52 pounds
60 pounds
120 pounds
explanation: Normal force = W × cos(θ) = 60 × cos(30°) = 60 × 0.866 ≈ 52 pounds. The component pulling the block down the ramp = W × sin(30°) = 60 × 0.5 = 30 pounds. The normal force is NOT equal to the full weight on a ramp, it is reduced by the cosine of the angle.

stem: A person pushes down at an angle on a lawnmower handle. The downward component of the pushing force increases the normal force on the mower's wheels. What effect does this have on friction?
choices: Friction decreases because the mower moves forward
Friction increases because friction depends on normal force
Friction is unchanged because only horizontal force causes motion
Friction is eliminated by the forward-directed component
explanation: Friction force equals the coefficient of friction times the normal force: f = mu x N. Adding a downward component of push increases the normal force the floor exerts upward on the mower wheels, and friction scales directly with that normal force. Choice A (decreases) reverses the relationship; pushing down increases, not decreases, the resistance. Choice C (unchanged) ignores that the vertical force component adds to the weight, raising the normal force. Choice D (eliminated) is physically impossible; no horizontal component can cancel friction. This is why pulling at an upward angle is easier than pushing at a downward angle.

stem: A 120-pound crate is on a frictionless surface. Two horizontal forces act on it: 50 pounds to the right and 30 pounds to the left. What is the net force and the resulting acceleration? (Use weight ÷ 32 ft/s² to find mass in slugs.)
choices: Net 20 pounds right; acceleration ≈ 5.3 ft/s²
Net 80 pounds right; acceleration ≈ 21 ft/s²
Net 20 pounds right; acceleration ≈ 2.5 ft/s²
Net 50 pounds right; no acceleration because friction balances it
explanation: Net force = 50 - 30 = 20 pounds to the right. Mass in slugs = weight / g = 120 / 32 = 3.75 slugs. Acceleration = F_net / m = 20 / 3.75 = 5.33 ft/s^2 to the right. Choice B (80 pounds; 21 ft/s^2) adds the two forces instead of subtracting them. Choice C (20 pounds; 2.5 ft/s^2) uses weight (120 lb) directly as mass instead of dividing by g, doubling the apparent mass. Choice D (50 pounds; no acceleration) misreads the surface as having friction; with no friction, the only horizontal forces are the two applied pushes.

stem: A 500-Newton engine rests on a steel workbench. The coefficient of static friction between the engine and bench is 0.3. A horizontal force of 160 N is applied to the engine. Does the engine move, and what is the maximum static friction force?
choices: Yes, the engine moves; maximum static friction was 150 N, which 160 N exceeds
Yes, the engine moves; maximum static friction was 160 N, which the applied force just equals
No, the engine stays put; maximum static friction was 150 N, but it grows to match the 160 N applied
No, the engine stays put; maximum static friction was 160 N opposing the applied force
explanation: Compute the static-friction ceiling: f_s,max = μ_s × N = 0.3 × 500 = 150 N. The applied force of 160 N exceeds that ceiling, so the engine begins to slide. Choice B (150 vs 160 is mistakenly read as equal) ignores the 10 N gap that triggers motion. Choice C is the most common misconception: students think static friction always rises to match whatever force is applied, but it actually caps at μ_s × N. Choice D treats kinetic friction as the answer at rest and uses the wrong number entirely; static friction never exceeds its ceiling.

stem: A block slides down a ramp at a constant speed. At constant speed, which of the following must be true?
choices: There is no friction force on the block
The component of gravity along the ramp exactly equals the kinetic friction force
The normal force equals the gravitational force
The coefficient of kinetic friction is zero
explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so the net force along the ramp is zero. The component of gravity pulling the block down the ramp (W sin theta) must be exactly canceled by kinetic friction (mu_k x N) acting up the ramp. Choice A (no friction) would leave gravity unopposed and the block would accelerate, not move at constant speed. Choice C (normal equals gravity) is true only on a flat surface; on a ramp, normal force equals W cos theta, less than full weight. Choice D (mu_k = 0) is equivalent to choice A and contradicts the constant-speed observation.

stem: A 40-kg box sits on a horizontal floor. Coefficient of static friction = 0.5, kinetic friction = 0.35. A horizontal force of 180 N is applied. What is the friction force and does the box move? (g = 10 m/s²)
choices: Friction = 200 N, box does not move
Friction = 180 N, box does not move
Maximum static friction = 200 N; 180 N < 200 N so box stays; friction = 180 N
Box moves; kinetic friction = 140 N
explanation: Normal force = mg = 40 × 10 = 400 N. Maximum static friction = 0.5 × 400 = 200 N. Applied force = 180 N < 200 N, so the box does NOT move. When stationary, static friction equals the applied force: 180 N. Kinetic friction (140 N) is only relevant if the box were sliding.

stem: A crate is being pushed across a rough floor at constant velocity with a horizontal force of 80 N. If the floor surface is then treated to reduce the coefficient of kinetic friction by half, and the same 80-N force is maintained, what happens to the crate?
choices: It continues at the same constant velocity
It decelerates and stops
It accelerates because the applied force now exceeds the reduced friction
It reverses direction
explanation: Originally, applied force (80 N) balanced friction (80 N) for constant velocity. Halving the coefficient of kinetic friction halves the friction force to 40 N while the applied force stays at 80 N. Net force = 80 - 40 = 40 N in the direction of motion. By Newton's Second Law, a = F_net / m > 0, so the crate accelerates. Choice A (same velocity) ignores the new force imbalance. Choice B (decelerates) reverses the direction of the net force; less friction means less resistance, not more. Choice D (reverses direction) would require a net force opposite to motion, which is not the case here.

stem: A 10-kg block rests on a 25-degree incline. The coefficient of static friction is 0.5. Will the block slide? (Use g = 10 m/s², sin 25° ≈ 0.42, cos 25° ≈ 0.91)
choices: Yes, because gravity down the ramp exceeds maximum static friction
No, because maximum static friction exceeds gravity's ramp component
It will slide at exactly constant velocity
Insufficient information without knowing the block's surface material
explanation: Gravity along ramp = mg sin 25° = 10 × 10 × 0.42 = 42 N. Normal force = mg cos 25° = 10 × 10 × 0.91 = 91 N. Maximum static friction = 0.5 × 91 = 45.5 N. Since 45.5 N > 42 N, static friction can fully oppose the sliding tendency. The block does not slide.

stem: A screw is a simple machine based on which other simple machine?
choices: Lever
Wheel and axle
Inclined plane wrapped in a helix
Pulley
explanation: A screw is geometrically an inclined plane wrapped helically around a cylinder. The pitch (distance between threads) is the height of the inclined plane, and the circumference traveled per rotation is its length. Choice A (lever) is unrelated; a screw does not rotate around a fixed fulcrum like a lever. Choice B (wheel and axle) is the closest second answer because screws are turned by a wheel-like head, but the underlying geometry that creates the linear advance is the spiral ramp itself, not a wheel-axle radius ratio. Choice D (pulley) does not match; pulleys use ropes and grooves, not threaded inclines.

stem: Pascal's principle states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is:
choices: absorbed by the fluid and converted to heat
transmitted equally in all directions throughout the fluid
reduced in proportion to the depth of the fluid
only transmitted upward in the fluid
explanation: Pascal's principle states that pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions and to every surface bounding the fluid. This is the foundation of hydraulic systems, brakes, and lifts. Choice A (absorbed and converted to heat) describes friction or compression losses, not the transmission of pressure. Choice C (reduced by depth) is true for static fluid pressure due to gravity but is not Pascal's principle; Pascal's law concerns the added pressure from external force. Choice D (only upward) is geometrically wrong; fluids transmit applied pressure in every direction at once.

stem: A frictionless ramp is 12 feet long and rises 3 feet vertically. What is its mechanical advantage?
explanation: Mechanical advantage of an inclined plane = length of slope / vertical height = 12 / 3 = 4. A longer ramp (relative to height) requires less force but the load must travel a greater distance. MA = 4 means you need 1/4 of the load's weight as effort force.

stem: A hydraulic system input piston has an area of 5 in² and the output piston has an area of 25 in². If the input force is 100 pounds, what force does the output piston exert?
choices: 20 pounds
100 pounds
500 pounds
2,500 pounds
explanation: Hydraulic force multiplication: F_out = F_in x (A_out / A_in) = 100 x (25 / 5) = 100 x 5 = 500 pounds. Choice A (20 pounds) inverts the area ratio and divides force by 5 instead of multiplying. Choice B (100 pounds) ignores the area difference entirely. Choice D (2,500 pounds) multiplies force by the output area alone (100 x 25), ignoring the input area in the denominator. Hydraulics multiply force by the ratio of areas; the input piston moves a longer distance with less force, the output piston moves a shorter distance with more force, conserving work.

stem: A ramp 20 feet long rises 5 feet. A 240-pound load is pushed up the ramp. What effort force is required (frictionless)?
choices: 12 pounds
40 pounds
48 pounds
60 pounds
explanation: MA = ramp length / height = 20 / 5 = 4. Effort = Load / MA = 240 / 4 = 60 pounds. A trap answer of 48 pounds corresponds to 240 / 5 (dividing by height instead of MA).

stem: A hydraulic brake system has an input piston area of 1 in² and the brake caliper piston area is 3 in². A driver applies 30 pounds of force at the brake pedal, which the booster amplifies by a factor of 4 before reaching the master cylinder. How much clamping force does the caliper exert?
choices: 30 pounds
90 pounds
120 pounds
360 pounds
explanation: Two stages of multiplication apply in series. Booster: 30 x 4 = 120 pounds at the master cylinder. Hydraulic area ratio: 120 x (3/1) = 360 pounds at the caliper. Choice A (30 pounds) ignores both stages. Choice B (90 pounds) applies only the hydraulic ratio to the bare pedal force (30 x 3 = 90), skipping the booster. Choice C (120 pounds) stops after the booster and ignores the hydraulic stage. When mechanical and hydraulic advantages chain together, you multiply their factors: total MA = 4 x 3 = 12, and final force = 30 x 12 = 360 pounds.

stem: A wedge has a length of 10 inches and a thickness (wide end) of 2 inches. You drive it under a 600-pound rock. How much driving force is needed to generate the lifting force (assuming MA fully transfers)?
choices: 60 pounds
120 pounds
300 pounds
600 pounds
explanation: Wedge MA = length / thickness = 10 / 2 = 5. Driving force = Load / MA = 600 / 5 = 120 pounds. Choice A (60 pounds) doubles the wedge MA to 10, perhaps using length / (thickness/2). Choice C (300 pounds) halves the load and ignores the MA, treating the wedge like a simple lever with MA = 2. Choice D (600 pounds) skips the wedge entirely and uses the load weight as the required force. A wedge converts a small driving force along its length into a much larger splitting force perpendicular to its motion, just like an inclined plane in mirror image.

stem: In a hydraulic system, the input piston moves down 10 inches with an area of 2 in². The output piston has an area of 8 in². How far does the output piston move upward?
choices: 1.25 inches
2.5 inches
5 inches
40 inches
explanation: Fluid volume is conserved: Volume in = Volume out. Input volume = 2 in² × 10 in = 20 in³. Output: 20 in³ = 8 in² × d. d = 20 / 8 = 2.5 inches. The output piston moves a shorter distance but with proportionally more force, the same trade-off as any mechanical advantage system.

stem: A loading ramp is 18 feet long and rises 6 feet. An 800-pound engine is pushed up the ramp with a steady force. How much work is done on the engine as it is raised the full 6 feet vertically? (Work = Force × Distance traveled; use the vertical height.)
choices: 800 foot-pounds
2,400 foot-pounds
4,800 foot-pounds
14,400 foot-pounds
explanation: Work done against gravity = weight × vertical height = 800 × 6 = 4,800 foot-pounds. The ramp's MA (18/6 = 3) reduces the effort force to 800/3 ≈ 267 pounds, but you push over 18 feet of ramp: 267 × 18 = 4,800 ft-lb, the same result, confirming conservation of energy. Work done on the load always equals weight × height, regardless of the path.

stem: A hydraulic press has an input cylinder of area 3 cm² and an output cylinder of area 75 cm². A technician pushes the input piston down 50 cm. What is the output force if the input force is 60 N?
explanation: Area ratio = 75 / 3 = 25. Output force = 60 × 25 = 1,500 N. The output piston moves 50 × (3/75) = 2 cm, short distance but large force. Output piston travel is inversely proportional to the area ratio, confirming energy conservation.

stem: An engine hoist uses a compound system: a 3:1 lever feeds into a 4-rope pulley block-and-tackle. An engine weighs 600 pounds. What is the minimum effort force required at the lever input to lift the engine?
choices: 50 pounds
200 pounds
400 pounds
600 pounds
explanation: When simple machines are combined in series, their mechanical advantages multiply: Total MA = 3 x 4 = 12. Effort = Load / Total MA = 600 / 12 = 50 pounds. Choice B (200 pounds) uses only the pulley MA of 3, ignoring the lever. Choice C (400 pounds) reduces the load by only 1.5, perhaps averaging the MAs (3+4)/2 instead of multiplying. Choice D (600 pounds) ignores both stages and uses the full engine weight. Compound machines trade input force for input distance: you pull through 12 times the distance the engine rises.

stem: A hydraulic system output piston has an area of 50 in². The system pressure is 200 psi. What force does the output piston exert?
choices: 4 pounds
250 pounds
10,000 pounds
1,000,000 pounds
explanation: Force = Pressure × Area = 200 psi × 50 in² = 10,000 pounds. This is the fundamental hydraulic force formula. The trap answer 250 pounds comes from adding (200 + 50) rather than multiplying, and 4 pounds comes from dividing (200 / 50).

stem: Newton's Second Law of Motion states that force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma). If a 2-kg object has an acceleration of 10 m/s², what is the net force on it?
choices: 5 N
explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = m x a = 2 kg x 10 m/s^2 = 20 N. Choice A (5 N) divides incorrectly (a / m / 2). Choice B (12 N) adds mass and acceleration (2 + 10), mixing incompatible units. Choice D (0.2 N) inverts the formula and divides mass by acceleration (2 / 10). Force is proportional to both mass and acceleration; the units (N = kg m/s^2) confirm the multiplicative relationship. Heavier objects need more force for the same acceleration; lighter objects accelerate more under the same force.

stem: A ball is thrown horizontally from a building ledge. At the instant it leaves the thrower's hand, which direction does gravity act on it?
choices: Horizontally, in the direction of throw
Upward, opposing the throw
Vertically downward, regardless of horizontal motion
At a 45-degree angle combining horizontal and vertical
explanation: Near Earth's surface, gravity acts vertically downward at 9.8 m/s^2 regardless of horizontal motion. The instant the ball leaves the hand, the only force on it (ignoring air resistance) is the downward pull of gravity. Choice A (horizontal in direction of throw) confuses initial velocity with the gravitational force; the throw gives velocity, not a continued force. Choice B (upward) reverses gravity's direction. Choice D (45 degrees) adds a horizontal component to gravity that does not exist; horizontal and vertical motions are independent in projectile motion, with gravity acting only on the vertical component.

stem: What is the momentum of a 4-kg object moving at 15 m/s?
choices: 3.75 kg·m/s
11 kg·m/s
60 kg·m/s
225 kg·m/s
explanation: Momentum p = mass × velocity = 4 kg × 15 m/s = 60 kg·m/s. Dividing instead of multiplying (4/15 ≈ 0.27 or 15/4 = 3.75) inverts the formula. Momentum is a vector quantity with units of kg·m/s.

stem: In a perfectly inelastic collision, two objects stick together after impact. What is conserved in this type of collision?
choices: Kinetic energy only
Momentum only
Both kinetic energy and momentum
Neither momentum nor kinetic energy
explanation: In any collision (elastic or inelastic), total momentum is conserved. In a perfectly inelastic collision, kinetic energy is NOT conserved, some is converted to heat, sound, and deformation. Elastic collisions conserve both momentum and kinetic energy.

stem: A rocket in space fires its engines and accelerates. Newton's Third Law explains this by stating that:
choices: Objects in space have no inertia and respond to any force
The exhaust gases are pushed backward, and the rocket is pushed forward by an equal and opposite reaction force
The rocket's mass decreases as fuel is burned, increasing speed automatically
Gravity pulls the rocket forward in the direction of acceleration
explanation: Newton's Third Law: for every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force. The rocket pushes exhaust gases backward (action); the gases push the rocket forward with an equal force (reaction). Because the action-reaction pair acts on the rocket and the gases (not on the surrounding air), thrust works in a vacuum. Choice A (no inertia in space) is wrong; objects retain their mass and inertia everywhere. Choice C (mass loss alone causes speed) ignores that a force is needed to accelerate the remaining mass. Choice D (gravity pulls forward) misidentifies the force; gravity pulls toward planetary masses, not in the direction of acceleration.

stem: A 3-kg object moving east at 8 m/s collides with a 3-kg object at rest. They stick together. What is the velocity of the combined mass immediately after the collision?
choices: 8 m/s east
4 m/s east
0 m/s
2 m/s east
explanation: Conservation of momentum: p_before = (3 × 8) + (3 × 0) = 24 kg·m/s. Combined mass = 6 kg. v_after = 24 / 6 = 4 m/s east. The combined mass is twice the original, so velocity is halved. This is a perfectly inelastic collision, kinetic energy is not conserved but momentum is.

stem: A ball is thrown upward and reaches its highest point. At that exact moment, what is the ball's velocity and what is its acceleration?
choices: Velocity = 0 m/s; acceleration = 0 m/s² (weightless at peak)
Velocity = 0 m/s; acceleration = 9.8 m/s² downward
Velocity = peak speed; acceleration = 9.8 m/s² upward
Velocity = 0 m/s; acceleration is upward until it reverses
explanation: At the highest point, vertical velocity is momentarily zero (the ball is neither moving up nor down). However, gravity still acts downward at 9.8 m/s² continuously, acceleration due to gravity never stops, regardless of the object's velocity. This is a common misconception: zero velocity does not mean zero acceleration.

stem: Two balls are dropped simultaneously from the same height: Ball A has a mass of 1 kg, Ball B has a mass of 5 kg. Ignoring air resistance, which hits the ground first?
choices: Ball A, because it is lighter and falls more easily
Ball B, because gravity exerts more force on it
They hit at the same time
It depends on the shape of the balls
explanation: Galileo's principle: in the absence of air resistance, all objects fall with the same gravitational acceleration (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²) regardless of mass. Although gravity exerts more force on Ball B, Ball B also has more inertia (F = ma → a = F/m, and F and m both scale with mass, so a = g for all). Both hit simultaneously.

stem: A stationary 8-kg bowling ball is struck by a 0.5-kg cue ball moving at 20 m/s. After the collision, the cue ball bounces straight back at 10 m/s. Using conservation of momentum, what is the approximate velocity of the bowling ball after the collision?
choices: 0.94 m/s in the original direction of the cue ball
1.875 m/s in the original direction
20 m/s in the original direction
10 m/s backward
explanation: Taking the cue ball's initial direction as positive: p_before = 0.5 × 20 + 8 × 0 = 10 kg·m/s. p_after = 0.5 × (−10) + 8 × v_bowling. 10 = −5 + 8v → 8v = 15 → v = 1.875 m/s in the original forward direction. The cue ball reverses direction (negative), transferring most of its momentum to the heavier ball.
